posts.title: Claude Code replaced my junior dev workflow
posts.hook:
I mass-deleted 14 GitHub repos last week.

Not because they failed. Because Claude Code made them irrelevant.
posts.body:
I mass-deleted 14 GitHub repos last week.

Not because they failed. Because Claude Code made them irrelevant.

Those repos were scaffolding tools, template generators, CLI utilities I'd built over the past year to speed up repetitive dev work. Each one took 2-4 hours to build and maintain.

Then I started using Claude Code with a proper CLAUDE.md and skills system. Now I describe what I want in plain English, it reads my codebase, and builds it in context.

The 14 repos weren't bad code. They were the right solution for a world where AI couldn't hold 200 files in working memory.

That world ended about 3 months ago.

What I'm seeing across every builder I talk to: the half-life of internal tooling is collapsing. The scripts you wrote in January might already be obsolete. Not because they broke, but because the cost of rebuilding from scratch dropped below the cost of maintaining what exists.

The shift isn't "AI writes code for you." It's that throwaway code is now cheaper than maintained code. That changes how you architect everything.
posts.cta: What tooling have you sunset since adopting coding agents?

remixes.label: cost-audit-angle
remixes.hook: I tracked every hour I spent maintaining internal dev tools last year. The number was embarrassing.
remixes.body:
I tracked every hour I spent maintaining internal dev tools last year. The number was embarrassing.

147 hours. That's nearly a full month of work spent updating template generators, fixing CLI utilities, and patching scaffolding scripts that broke every time a dependency updated.

These weren't bad tools. They saved me real time when I built them. But each one came with a maintenance tax I never budgeted for. A new Node version breaks the CLI. A framework update changes the boilerplate. Suddenly I'm debugging my debugging tools.

When I set up Claude Code with a structured skills system and a proper CLAUDE.md, something clicked. I wasn't replacing individual tools. I was replacing the entire category of "things I build to avoid repetition."

Now instead of maintaining a repo that generates Next.js starter projects, I describe the project shape in plain English. Instead of a custom migration script, I explain what needs to move and where. The agent handles the execution. I handle the decisions.

The 147 hours aren't coming back. But I stopped the bleeding. Every dev tool I delete is time I'm not spending on tool maintenance and time I'm spending on actual product work.

The uncomfortable question: how many of your internal tools exist because automation wasn't good enough yet?
remixes.cta: How many hours a month do you spend maintaining tools that were supposed to save you time?

remixes.label: identity-shift-narrative
remixes.hook: I used to introduce myself as someone who builds developer tools. I can't say that anymore.
remixes.body:
I used to introduce myself as someone who builds developer tools. I can't say that anymore.

Not because I stopped caring about developer experience. Because the definition of "tool" changed underneath me.

For years, my edge was that I could spin up a CLI or template generator faster than most people could write the spec. I had a personal ecosystem of 14+ repos. Scaffolders, linters, migration utilities, deploy scripts. Each one represented a solved problem I never wanted to solve again.

Then coding agents got good enough to solve those problems on the fly. Not approximately. Actually solve them, with context about my specific codebase, my conventions, my preferences.

The shift wasn't gradual. I went from "I should build a tool for this" to "I should just tell Claude Code what I want" in about two weeks. Once you internalize that the agent has your entire project context, building a standalone tool feels like writing a letter when you could just call someone.

I deleted all 14 repos in one sitting. Not dramatically. Just practically. They were dead weight.

The new skill isn't building tools. It's describing problems clearly enough that an agent can build the right thing every time. That's a fundamentally different muscle.

My title didn't change. But my job did.
remixes.cta: Has your actual day-to-day shifted faster than your job title? What changed?

posts.title: Outbound automation is a race to the bottom
posts.hook: Every outbound team is buying the same Apollo list, running the same Instantly sequences, and wondering why reply rates are dropping.
posts.body:
Every outbound team is buying the same Apollo list, running the same Instantly sequences, and wondering why reply rates are dropping.

I ran outbound the old way for years. 200+ emails a day from primary domains with no warmup on SalesLoft. Built buying committees manually in Salesforce. It was brutal, but it worked because most teams weren't doing it at all.

Now everyone has the same playbook. Same data providers. Same AI-written copy. Same send schedules.

The teams winning outbound in 2026 aren't automating harder. They're automating differently.

Three patterns I'm seeing work:

✅ Signal-first targeting. Don't start with a list. Start with a trigger. Job posting, tech stack change, funding round. Then build the list from the signal.

✅ Research depth over send volume. One deeply personalized email that references their actual stack beats 50 templated ones.

✅ Owned channels as the conversion layer. Outbound gets attention. Your content, community, or tool converts it. Email alone doesn't close anymore.

The automation layer matters less than the intelligence layer feeding it.
posts.cta: What's your highest-converting outbound signal right now?

remixes.label: signal-vs-volume
remixes.hook: The best outbound email I sent last quarter had no template, no sequence, and no automation tool behind it.
remixes.body:
The best outbound email I sent last quarter had no template, no sequence, and no automation tool behind it.

It was one email. Written by hand. Sent to one person. It referenced a specific podcast episode they'd been on, a problem they mentioned, and a concrete way I could help.

It got a reply in 11 minutes.

Meanwhile, my 200-email Instantly sequence from the same week generated exactly zero meetings. Same ICP. Same offer. Same person writing the copy. The only difference was signal density.

The outbound industry has an addiction to volume. More sends. More sequences. More tools to automate the sending of more messages that nobody wants to receive. Every team is pulling from the same data providers, using the same templates with the same personalization tokens, and flooding the same inboxes.

Reply rates aren't dropping because buyers got harder to reach. They're dropping because every message looks identical. When everyone runs the same playbook, the playbook stops working. That's not a theory. That's how markets saturate.

The teams I see winning at outbound right now are doing less volume with more research per touch. They're reading earnings calls, scanning LinkedIn activity, monitoring job postings for buying signals. Then they write fewer, better messages.

Volume was the strategy when most teams weren't doing outbound. Now that everyone is, volume is just noise.
remixes.cta: When was the last time a mass outbound sequence actually outperformed a handful of researched, manual emails for you?

remixes.label: buyer-perspective-flip
remixes.hook: I counted my inbox last Tuesday. 23 cold emails. Same structure. Same personalization tokens. I deleted all of them.
remixes.body:
I counted my inbox last Tuesday. 23 cold emails. Same structure. Same personalization tokens. I deleted all of them.

Every single one followed the same formula: compliment + pain point assumption + vague value prop + calendar link. Half of them even had the same "I noticed you" opener. Three referenced the same blog post I wrote, clearly pulled from the same enrichment tool.

This is what outbound looks like from the buyer's side. And it explains why reply rates are cratering across the industry.

I say this as someone who ran high-volume outbound for years. 200+ emails daily from primary domains on SalesLoft. Built buying committees manually in Salesforce. It worked because most competitors weren't doing it. The bar was showing up at all.

Now the bar is being different from the other 22 cold emails that landed in the same inbox that morning. Same Apollo data. Same Instantly sequences. Same ChatGPT-generated "personalization." The tools democratized access to volume, which made volume worthless.

The shift I'm making: treating outbound like content, not like a numbers game. Research the person. Understand their actual situation. Write something they'd actually want to read. Send fewer messages that deserve replies instead of more messages that deserve the trash folder.

Buyers didn't get harder to reach. They got buried under a mountain of identical pitches.
remixes.cta: Be honest: would you reply to your own outbound sequences if they landed in your inbox?

Here's the pattern I'm seeing across every outbound team I work with: tools are converging faster than strategies are differentiating.

Everyone has access to Apollo, Instantly, Clay, and ChatGPT for personalization. The data is the same. The sequences are the same. The personalization tokens are the same. When your entire competitive advantage is operational efficiency at sending emails, and every competitor has the same operations, you've commoditized yourself.

My recommendation: cut your send volume by 80% and reallocate that time to signal research. Here's what actually moves reply rates right now.

First, monitor job postings in your ICP accounts. A new VP hire signals budget and mandate. Second, track LinkedIn engagement patterns. When a prospect starts commenting on topics adjacent to your solution, that's a timing signal. Third, read their company's recent earnings calls or press releases. Reference something specific and recent.

The math works. Ten researched emails with a 30% reply rate produce three conversations. Two hundred templated emails with a 1.5% reply rate produce three conversations. Same output, but the researched emails attract higher-quality prospects who are actually in a buying cycle.

Stop optimizing the assembly line. Start investing in signal intelligence. The teams winning outbound right now aren't sending more. They're knowing more before they send.
tones.provocateur:
Your outbound stack is a commodity and you're paying premium prices for it.

Apollo, Instantly, Clay, ChatGPT personalization. Congrats, you and 50,000 other B2B companies built the exact same machine. You're all pulling from the same database, writing the same "I noticed you" openers, and running the same 5-step sequences. Then you sit in pipeline reviews wondering why reply rates dropped 40% year over year.

The answer is obvious: you're not doing outbound. You're participating in a distributed spam operation that happens to target business email addresses.

I ran this playbook myself. 200+ emails a day from primary domains. No warmup. SalesLoft sequences firing constantly. It worked in 2021 because most teams were still cold calling or waiting for inbound. The competitive advantage was literally just showing up in someone's inbox.

That advantage is gone. Permanently. Every SDR team on the planet now has the same playbook, the same tools, and the same data. You can't out-volume your way out of a volume problem.

The teams actually booking meetings are doing something nobody wants to hear: less automation, more research, fewer sends, better signals. Manual work. The thing everyone bought these tools to avoid.

Your reply rate isn't a tool problem. It's a differentiation problem. And no new software is going to fix that.

posts.title: RevOps is an engineering problem
posts.hook: RevOps teams keep hiring analysts when they need engineers.
posts.body:
RevOps teams keep hiring analysts when they need engineers.

I've built GTM infrastructure for enough companies now to see the pattern. The bottleneck is never "we need more dashboards." It's "our systems don't talk to each other and nobody can fix the plumbing."

HubSpot workflows break silently. Salesforce automations conflict. The enrichment pipeline dumps garbage into the CRM because someone changed a field label three months ago and nobody noticed.

Analysts find the problems. Engineers fix the systems that create them.

The best RevOps setups I've seen treat their stack like a codebase. Version-controlled workflow logic. Automated testing on enrichment pipelines. Monitoring that catches data quality issues before they hit a dashboard.

One team I worked with cut their "bad data" tickets by 80% by adding a single validation layer between Clay and their CRM. Took 4 hours to build. They'd been manually cleaning records for 6 months.

The gap between RevOps-as-reporting and RevOps-as-engineering is where most teams lose pipeline without ever knowing it.
posts.cta: Is your RevOps team building systems or building reports?

inspired_by.author: Sarah Chen
inspired_by.summary: Explains revenue operations as the glue between sales, marketing, and customer success, with a focus on unified tech stacks. Details how RevOps reduced CAC by 40% through process optimization and cross-functional alignment. Provides actionable steps for building a RevOps function from scratch.

remixes.label: horror-story-angle
remixes.hook: A HubSpot workflow silently broke in March. Nobody noticed until $340K in pipeline went dark for six weeks.
remixes.body:
A HubSpot workflow silently broke in March. Nobody noticed until $340K in pipeline went dark for six weeks.

The failure was embarrassingly simple. Someone renamed a lifecycle stage property value. The workflow that routed leads to the right AE kept running without errors. It just stopped matching anyone. Leads came in, hit the workflow, and went nowhere.

No alerts fired. No dashboards caught it. The RevOps team was busy building a board-ready pipeline report and didn't notice the routing had failed until an AE asked why they hadn't gotten a new lead in over a month.

This is the RevOps problem nobody talks about. The team is staffed for analysis and reporting. They can build you a beautiful dashboard that shows pipeline velocity by segment. But when the underlying plumbing breaks, they don't have the engineering skills to build monitoring, write automated tests, or create failsafes.

I've seen this pattern in six companies now. The RevOps stack is held together by manual processes, tribal knowledge, and workflows that nobody fully understands. It works until it doesn't. And when it breaks, the damage is silent and cumulative.

RevOps needs engineers who can write integration tests for CRM workflows, build alerting for data quality issues, and treat the GTM infrastructure like production software. Because that's what it is.

Dashboards don't catch broken pipes. Monitoring does.
remixes.cta: When was the last time you audited your CRM workflows for silent failures?

remixes.label: hiring-blueprint
remixes.hook: Stop posting "RevOps Analyst" roles. Post "GTM Systems Engineer" and watch the quality of your pipeline change.
remixes.body:
Stop posting "RevOps Analyst" roles. Post "GTM Systems Engineer" and watch the quality of your pipeline change.

The job description signals who applies. When you hire for "RevOps Analyst," you get someone who's great at building reports in Salesforce, maintaining dashboards, and answering questions about pipeline data. That's valuable. But it's not what's breaking.

What's breaking is the infrastructure underneath those reports. The HubSpot-to-Salesforce sync that drops records when field mappings change. The enrichment pipeline that writes over clean data with garbage because there's no validation layer. The Slack alerts that stopped firing three months ago and nobody noticed.

These are engineering problems. They need engineering solutions. Version-controlled automation logic. Integration tests for CRM workflows. Monitoring and alerting for data quality. API-first architecture instead of point-and-click connectors that break silently.

The companies I've worked with that figured this out didn't restructure their whole RevOps team. They added one person who could write code, understand APIs, and think about systems reliability. That single hire fixed more pipeline issues in their first quarter than the entire analyst team had identified in the prior year.

Not because the analysts weren't good. Because they were solving reporting problems when the actual bottleneck was plumbing problems.

The role you're missing isn't another analyst. It's someone who treats your GTM stack like the production system it is.
remixes.cta: Does your RevOps team have someone who can actually write code? Not formulas. Code.

tones.advisor:
If your RevOps team is mostly analysts and operations generalists, you have a structural gap that's costing you pipeline without anyone realizing it.

Here's what I see consistently: companies invest heavily in CRM platforms, enrichment tools, and automation builders. They hire smart people to configure these systems and build reports. But nobody on the team can write code, build integrations from scratch, or implement the kind of monitoring that catches silent failures.

The result is predictable. Workflows break without alerting. Data quality degrades gradually. Systems that should talk to each other are connected by fragile point-and-click automations that nobody fully understands.

My recommendation is straightforward. Your next RevOps hire should be an engineer, not an analyst. Someone who can write integration tests for your CRM workflows. Someone who can build real-time monitoring for data quality issues. Someone who treats your GTM infrastructure with the same rigor a backend team applies to production systems.

This doesn't mean replacing your analysts. Their work matters. But analysis built on broken plumbing produces misleading results. Fix the plumbing first.

The companies I've seen make this shift typically see results within one quarter. Fewer silent failures, cleaner data, more reliable pipeline numbers. The ROI on one systems engineer often exceeds the ROI of two additional analysts, simply because they're solving the right category of problem.
tones.provocateur:
RevOps is the only function in a modern company where "building dashboards" counts as delivering value while the entire data pipeline underneath is on fire.

I'm not being dramatic. I've audited GTM infrastructure at companies with RevOps teams of 5+ people. Beautiful Salesforce dashboards. Gorgeous Tableau reports. Pipeline reviews that would make a CFO weep with joy.

Meanwhile, the HubSpot-to-Salesforce sync is dropping 8% of records. The enrichment pipeline is overwriting clean data with outdated ZoomInfo pulls. Lead routing has three competing workflows that nobody fully understands because the person who built two of them left last year.

And the response? "We need to hire another analyst."

No. You need to hire someone who can write code. Not configure tools. Not build workflows in a visual editor. Actually write code that tests your integrations, monitors your data quality, and alerts you when something breaks before it costs you six figures in lost pipeline.

RevOps has been treated like a reporting function when it's actually an engineering function. The CRM is a production system. The enrichment pipeline is a production system. Lead routing is a production system. They should all have tests, monitoring, and version control.

But sure, build another dashboard. That'll fix it.

posts.title: Solo builders are eating agencies
posts.hook: A solo founder with Claude Code, Vercel, and a Stripe account is now more dangerous than a 15-person agency.
posts.body:
A solo founder with Claude Code, Vercel, and a Stripe account is now more dangerous than a 15-person agency.

Not a hot take. Just math.

Agency model: sell the engagement, staff the project, manage the team, deliver in 6-8 weeks, hope the client doesn't churn before you break even on acquisition cost.

Solo builder model: find the problem, ship the solution in a weekend, charge monthly, iterate based on direct user feedback.

I watched Pieter Levels ship a product to 12k likes on a single post. No team. No funding round. No sprint planning meetings.

The pattern repeating across every builder I follow: the ones shipping fastest aren't managing people. They're managing AI agents. The coordination cost dropped to near zero when your "team" is a Claude Code session with good context files.

This doesn't mean agencies die. But the ones that survive will look more like 3 engineers with AI multipliers than 15 people with Jira boards.

The leverage changed. The org chart hasn't caught up yet.
posts.cta: Are you building with a team or building with agents? Genuinely curious how the split is going.

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shared his latest product launch as a solo developer, detailing the tech stack used and initial user feedback after shipping on Product Hunt. He emphasized the importance of building in public to attract early users and iterate quickly. The post garnered significant engagement from the indie hacker community.

remixes.label: economics-breakdown
remixes.hook: I priced out what it would cost to deliver my last project through an agency. The number was 14x what I charged.
remixes.body:
I priced out what it would cost to deliver my last project through an agency. The number was 14x what I charged.

The project: a custom landing page system with CRM integration, automated email sequences, and a real-time analytics dashboard. I shipped it in a weekend. Charged $2,400.

An agency doing the same scope would need a project manager, a designer, a frontend dev, a backend dev, and an account manager. Minimum 4-6 week timeline. Conservative estimate: $30,000-35,000.

Same deliverable. Same quality. One person with coding agents versus five people with Jira tickets.

This isn't because agencies are inefficient for the sake of being inefficient. It's because their cost structure requires overhead that a solo builder simply doesn't have. Every agency dollar carries the weight of office space, management layers, sales commissions, and coordination costs. Roughly 60-70% of what a client pays goes to everything except the actual work.

Coding agents didn't just make solo builders faster. They eliminated the primary reason agencies existed: you needed a team to cover all the skill gaps. Now one person with Claude Code can handle frontend, backend, DevOps, and copywriting in a single sitting.

The agencies that survive will be the ones that restructure around this reality. Smaller teams, higher leverage per person, pricing based on outcomes instead of hours.

The ones still staffing projects like it's 2022 are going to have a rough year.
remixes.cta: If you're running an agency, how are you adapting your model? If you're a solo builder, what's your pricing strategy?

remixes.label: client-trust-angle
remixes.hook: My clients don't know I work alone. And that's exactly the point.
remixes.body:
My clients don't know I work alone. And that's exactly the point.

They see deliverables shipping faster than any agency they've worked with. Bug fixes deployed the same day they're reported. Feature requests turned around in hours instead of sprint cycles. Responsive communication with no account manager bottleneck.

What they don't see is that it's one person and a coding agent in a terminal.

This isn't a flex about being a 10x developer. I'm not. What I am is someone who figured out that the coordination overhead of a team was the actual bottleneck in most client projects. Not the coding. Not the design. The meetings, the handoffs, the sprint ceremonies, the ticket management.

Remove the coordination layer and the speed increase is almost absurd. A feature that takes an agency two sprints to spec, assign, build, review, and deploy takes me an afternoon. Not because I skip steps, but because there are no handoff delays between steps.

The trust angle matters here too. When a client works with an agency, they trust the brand and hope they get a good team. When they work with a solo builder, they trust the person. That direct relationship means faster feedback loops, better understanding of their business, and fewer things lost in translation.

The agency model was built for a world where one person couldn't cover enough ground. That constraint is gone.

Solo builders who figure out the trust and positioning piece are going to eat an uncomfortable amount of agency market share over the next two years.
remixes.cta: What's the biggest advantage you've found working solo versus working with/at an agency?

The leverage changed. The org chart hasn't caught up yet.

posts.title: AI SDK changed how I think about backends
posts.hook:
I stopped building traditional API routes 4 months ago.

Everything is a streaming function now.
posts.body:
I stopped building traditional API routes 4 months ago.

Everything is a streaming function now.

Vercel's AI SDK quietly shifted how I architect apps. Instead of request/response endpoints that return JSON blobs, I'm building streaming pipelines that return structured data incrementally.

The UX difference is massive. Users see results appearing in real-time instead of staring at a spinner. But the bigger win is on the backend.

Streaming functions naturally decompose into steps. Each step is independently testable. Error handling becomes granular. You can fail on step 3 and still deliver steps 1 and 2.

I'm running a GTM automation stack where the enrichment pipeline streams results as each provider responds. Clay data comes back first, then web scraping, then AI classification. The user watches their list build in real-time instead of waiting 45 seconds for a batch response.

Traditional REST made sense when compute was request-shaped. AI workloads aren't. They're streams. Building around that instead of fighting it simplified my entire stack.

The infrastructure layer finally matches the workload pattern.
posts.cta: Anyone else moving to streaming-first architecture? What patterns are you finding?

remixes.label: before-after-architecture
remixes.hook: My backend used to be 40 API routes returning JSON. Now it's 6 streaming functions and the app feels completely different.
remixes.body:
My backend used to be 40 API routes returning JSON. Now it's 6 streaming functions and the app feels completely different.

The consolidation happened gradually. I started using Vercel's AI SDK for one feature, a chat interface that needed streaming responses. Standard use case. But the patterns I learned there started bleeding into everything else.

Search results? Stream them as they resolve instead of waiting for all 20 to load. Dashboard metrics? Stream each widget's data independently so the fast queries render instantly while the slow ones catch up. Form submissions? Stream the validation results and processing status instead of showing a generic loading state.

The architecture shift is subtle but significant. Traditional request/response treats every interaction as a transaction: send request, wait, receive complete response. Streaming treats every interaction as a conversation: start responding immediately, send data as it becomes available, signal when you're done.

The technical implementation is straightforward with the AI SDK. The mental model shift is what takes time. You stop thinking about endpoints that return objects and start thinking about pipelines that emit events. Error handling changes. Loading states change. The entire UX layer changes because you're no longer designing around "waiting for the server."

I went from 40 routes because each one returned a specific JSON shape to 6 streaming functions because streams are composable. Multiple data types flow through the same pipeline. The code got simpler, not more complex.

Four months in, I can't imagine going back to request/response for anything user-facing.
remixes.cta: If you've tried streaming-first architecture, what was the first non-AI feature you applied it to?

remixes.label: ux-impact-angle
remixes.hook: My users used to complain about load times. They stopped complaining, but nothing got faster. The data just arrives differently now.
remixes.body:
My users used to complain about load times. They stopped complaining, but nothing got faster. The data just arrives differently now.

Same database. Same queries. Same server. The only thing that changed was switching from traditional API routes that return complete JSON responses to streaming functions that send data incrementally.

The actual time from request to complete data hasn't changed much. But the perceived performance is dramatically better. Instead of staring at a spinner for 1.2 seconds while the server assembles a full response, users see the first results appear in 150ms. The rest fills in over the next second.

This is the part that surprised me about Vercel's AI SDK. Everyone talks about it in the context of chat and AI features. But the streaming primitives work for any data. Search results. Dashboard metrics. Form processing. Anything where you can start sending partial data before the full response is ready.

The pattern is simple. Instead of building an endpoint that gathers all the data, formats it, and sends one response, you build a function that starts emitting structured chunks immediately. Each chunk is a complete, usable piece of the response. The client renders them as they arrive.

The backend changes were minimal. The frontend changes were significant, because every component that previously expected a complete data object now handles progressive updates. But once you build the pattern once, it applies everywhere.

The counterintuitive result: my app didn't get faster. It just stopped feeling slow. And that turns out to be what users actually care about.
remixes.cta: What's the biggest UX win you've gotten from an architecture change that didn't actually improve raw performance?

posts.title: Content clusters killed our blog traffic
posts.hook: We followed the "topic cluster" SEO playbook exactly. Traffic dropped 40% in 3 months.
posts.body:
We followed the "topic cluster" SEO playbook exactly. Traffic dropped 40% in 3 months.

Pillar page. 12 supporting articles. Internal linking structure. Keyword mapping. The whole playbook from every B2B content strategy post you've ever read.

What actually happened: we cannibalized our own rankings. Google couldn't figure out which of our 4 articles about "enrichment workflows" to rank, so it ranked none of them.

The fix was counterintuitive. We consolidated. Fewer pages, more depth per page. One definitive piece on enrichment workflows instead of four thin ones trying to hit slightly different keyword variations.

The page that replaced all four now ranks top 3 for the primary keyword and picks up long-tail variations naturally.

Content strategy in 2026 isn't about volume or clusters. It's about building the single best resource for a topic and earning the right to rank for everything around it.

The teams still churning out 8 blog posts a month to "build topical authority" are feeding Google content it doesn't need. Write less. Go deeper. Actually teach something.
posts.cta: Has anyone else seen topic clusters backfire? Curious if this is widespread or just our experience.

inspired_by.author: Mike Volpe
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down content strategy for B2B SaaS, advocating for topic clusters over standalone posts to boost SEO and lead gen. Shares metrics from HubSpot era showing 3x engagement lift. Recommends tools like Ahrefs for gap analysis in competitive landscapes.

remixes.label: diagnostic-autopsy
remixes.hook: We wrote 12 articles about the same topic and wondered why Google got confused.
remixes.body:
We wrote 12 articles about the same topic and wondered why Google got confused.

The content cluster model assumes Google will neatly organize your internal hierarchy. Pillar page at the top, supporting posts feeding authority upward. Clean and logical.

Except Google doesn't read your content strategy deck. It sees 4 pages competing for "enrichment workflows" and picks the worst one. Or none of them.

Our traffic dropped 40% in a quarter. Not because the content was bad. Because we'd built a keyword cannibalization engine and called it a strategy.

The fix wasn't more content. It was less. We consolidated overlapping articles, killed three pages entirely, and redirected them to a single comprehensive piece. Within 6 weeks, that one page ranked higher than any of the originals.

The lesson: topic clusters work when each piece targets genuinely distinct intent. The moment two articles answer the same question differently, you're competing with yourself.

Before you build your next cluster, search your own domain for the target keyword. If something already ranks, don't write a new page. Make the existing one better.
remixes.cta: How many pages on your site are competing for the same keyword right now? Might be worth checking.

remixes.label: counterintuitive-win
remixes.hook: We deleted 8 blog posts and our organic traffic went up 35%.
remixes.body:
We deleted 8 blog posts and our organic traffic went up 35%.

Sounds backwards. Every content marketer knows you're supposed to publish more, not less. But we'd been running the topic cluster playbook for two quarters and our rankings were actually declining.

The problem was subtle. We had a pillar page on data enrichment, plus supporting articles on enrichment tools, enrichment workflows, enrichment best practices, and enrichment for sales teams. Google saw overlap everywhere.

Instead of figuring out which page to rank, it ranked none of them well. Classic cannibalization, but we didn't catch it because each article had different keywords in the title.

So we did something uncomfortable. We audited every cluster, identified articles with overlapping search intent (not just overlapping keywords), and merged or killed the redundant ones.

The remaining pages got stronger. More backlinks pointing to fewer URLs. Clearer topical authority. Less internal competition.

The content strategy world has a volume addiction. More posts, more clusters, more keywords. Sometimes the highest ROI move is a spreadsheet audit and a 301 redirect.
remixes.cta: When was the last time you pruned your content instead of adding to it?

posts.title: The infrastructure layer nobody talks about
posts.hook: AI agents are only as good as the context you feed them. Everyone's building agents. Almost nobody's building the context layer.
posts.body:
AI agents are only as good as the context you feed them. Everyone's building agents. Almost nobody's building the context layer.

I run Claude Code sessions with a CLAUDE.md file, skills system, voice DNA, and structured memory. The agent reads all of it before doing anything. Total context overhead: maybe 30 seconds at session start.

The output quality difference between "raw Claude" and "Claude with 2000 lines of structured context" is not incremental. It's a different tool.

But here's what I keep seeing. Teams buy an AI coding agent, point it at their repo with zero context files, get mediocre results, and conclude "AI agents aren't ready."

The agent was ready. The infrastructure wasn't.

Same pattern in GTM. Teams plug AI into their outbound sequence with no enrichment layer, no signal data, no ICP definition. The AI writes generic emails because it was given generic inputs.

The moat isn't the model. It's not the framework. It's the structured context layer that makes any model perform 10x better on your specific problem.

Infrastructure is boring. It's also the entire game.
posts.cta: What does your AI context layer look like? Or are you still running agents without one?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Analyzes the rise of AI infrastructure platforms like Together AI and Pinecone for scaling agent deployments. Argues that automation in infra will be key to enterprise adoption of multi-agent systems. References recent benchmarks showing 3x efficiency gains.

remixes.label: behind-the-curtain
remixes.hook: My AI agent reads 47 files before it writes a single line of code. That's not overhead. That's the product.
remixes.body:
My AI agent reads 47 files before it writes a single line of code. That's not overhead. That's the product.

Every Claude Code session I run starts with: a CLAUDE.md instruction file, a skills directory, voice DNA rules, structured memory from past sessions, and a context handoff from whatever I was doing last time.

Total startup cost: 30 seconds. Total impact on output quality: night and day.

Raw Claude is impressive. Claude with context about my codebase, my preferences, my voice, my current projects, and my past mistakes is a different tool entirely. It doesn't ask questions I've already answered. It doesn't suggest approaches I've already rejected. It remembers what we tried last Tuesday.

This is the part most people skip. They install an AI tool, prompt it from scratch every time, and wonder why the output feels generic.

The context layer isn't glamorous. It's markdown files, JSON configs, and naming conventions. No fancy orchestration framework. No vector database. Just structured text that the agent reads on boot.

But it's the difference between hiring a contractor who shows up every day with amnesia and one who reviewed the project notes on the drive over.
remixes.cta: What persistent context do you give your AI tools? Or does every session start from zero?

remixes.label: hot-take-inversion
remixes.hook: Stop building AI agents. Start building the memory layer they'll eventually need.
remixes.body:
Stop building AI agents. Start building the memory layer they'll eventually need.

The agent frameworks will change. The models will improve. The orchestration patterns that seem cutting-edge right now will look quaint in 18 months.

But the context layer, the structured knowledge about your business, your preferences, your workflows, your past decisions, that compounds.

I've been running Claude Code with a layered context system: instruction files, skill definitions, voice rules, session memory, and handoff documents. None of it is technically complex. It's markdown and JSON. The value isn't in the format. It's in the accumulation.

Every session my agent gets smarter because it has more context from previous sessions. Not smarter in the model sense. Smarter in the "knows my situation" sense.

The teams I see getting the most out of AI aren't the ones with the fanciest agent architectures. They're the ones who've invested in structured context. Company wikis that agents can read. Decision logs. Preference files. Project histories.

Agents are commoditizing fast. The context layer is the moat. It's specific to your organization, it improves over time, and it makes every agent you plug into it dramatically more useful.

Build the context, and the agents will come.

Here's what I mean. When I run Claude Code, the agent reads a CLAUDE.md instruction file, a skills directory, voice DNA rules, structured memory, and a context handoff from my previous session. All before it processes a single prompt. Takes about 30 seconds.

The output quality gap between a raw model and a model with this context is substantial. The agent doesn't repeat past mistakes. It knows my preferences. It understands the codebase conventions. It picks up where the last session left off.

My recommendation: start building your context layer now, even if it's simple. A markdown file with your coding conventions. A JSON file with project context. A memory system that persists decisions across sessions.

You don't need a vector database or a complex RAG pipeline. Structured plaintext that the agent reads at startup covers 80% of the value. The sophistication can come later.

The teams getting the best results from AI agents aren't using better models. They're feeding better context. That's the variable most people underinvest in.
tones.provocateur:
Everyone's building agents. Nobody's building the part that actually matters.

You can swap models every quarter. You can migrate between agent frameworks in a weekend. But the context layer, the structured knowledge that makes an agent actually useful for your specific situation, that takes months to build and can't be copied.

I watch people spin up AI agents with zero persistent context and then complain the output is generic. Of course it's generic. You gave it nothing specific to work with.

My Claude Code sessions start by reading 47 files of context. Instructions, skills, memory, voice rules, handoff notes. The agent knows my codebase, my preferences, my past mistakes, and what I was working on yesterday.

Is that overkill? The output quality says no.

: most "AI-powered" products right now are just thin wrappers around a model with no context. The moat isn't the agent. It's the knowledge base the agent sits on top of. And almost nobody is building that part because it's boring. It's markdown files and naming conventions. It doesn't demo well.

But it's the whole game.

posts.title: Career pivots look different now
posts.hook:
The career pivot used to mean: quit, learn for 6 months, job hunt for 3, land somewhere entry-level.

Now it means: keep your job, ship something on the side, let the product be your resume.
posts.body:
The career pivot used to mean: quit, learn for 6 months, job hunt for 3, land somewhere entry-level.

Now it means: keep your job, ship something on the side, let the product be your resume.

I've been watching the builder community closely. Arvid Kahl, Daniel Vassallo, Sahil Lavingia... different paths, same pattern. They didn't apply for their next role. They built it.

The economics shifted because the cost to ship dropped through the floor. Vercel deploys for free. Claude Code writes the boilerplate. Stripe handles payments. You can go from idea to revenue in a weekend if the problem is clear enough.

My own pivot went from SDR grinding 200 emails a day to building the systems that replace that grind. I didn't plan a "transition." I just started automating my own job and realized the automation was more valuable than the job.

The best career advice I can give anyone in 2026: stop optimizing your resume. Start shipping things. Even small things. A tool that solves one problem for 50 people teaches you more about building a career than any certification.

The resume gets you interviews. The shipped product gets you options.
posts.cta: What's the smallest thing you've shipped that changed your career trajectory?

remixes.label: economics-breakdown
remixes.hook: The cost of a career pivot dropped by 90%. Most people haven't updated their mental model.
remixes.body:
The cost of a career pivot dropped by 90%. Most people haven't updated their mental model.

Five years ago, switching careers meant: save 6 months of runway, enroll in a bootcamp or degree program, spend months applying for entry-level roles, take a pay cut, and hope it works out. Total cost: $50K+ and a year of your life.

Today: keep your job, use AI tools to ship a side project in evenings and weekends, let the project be your portfolio, and attract opportunities instead of applying for them.

The builders I watch doing this aren't following a traditional path. They're not getting hired. They're getting discovered. Someone sees their project on Twitter or GitHub, checks their profile, and reaches out.

The shift happened because the tools changed. You can build a functional web app in a weekend with AI assistance. You can launch a SaaS product as a solo founder. You can produce content that demonstrates expertise instead of just claiming it on a resume.

The old pivot required permission from gatekeepers: hiring managers, recruiters, admissions committees. The new pivot requires permission from nobody. Build something. Put it in public. Let the work speak.

The risk didn't disappear. But the minimum viable bet got dramatically smaller.
remixes.cta: What's the smallest project you could ship this month that shows what you're capable of?

remixes.label: pattern-recognition
remixes.hook: I keep seeing the same career move work over and over. Ship something small, share it publicly, get pulled into your next role.
remixes.body:
I keep seeing the same career move work over and over. Ship something small, share it publicly, get pulled into your next role.

Not apply. Not network. Get pulled.

Arvid Kahl built and sold a SaaS, then built an audience around what he learned. Daniel Vassallo left Amazon, shipped small bets publicly, and created an entirely new career. Sahil Lavingia built Gumroad, wrote about the journey, and became the face of a movement.

Different paths, same underlying pattern: they built something visible, shared the process, and let the work create opportunities.

This isn't just a founder playbook. I see engineers doing it by open-sourcing tools. Designers doing it by sharing case studies. Marketers doing it by publishing campaigns with real numbers.

The common thread: proof of work over proof of credentials.

A resume says "I can do this." A shipped project says "I already did this, here's the link." The second one wins every time.

The barrier used to be cost. Building and launching something took months and thousands of dollars. Now you can ship a working prototype in a weekend. The economics of the career pivot fundamentally changed when the cost of building dropped to near zero.

The only remaining cost is the willingness to put your work in public.
remixes.cta: What have you shipped recently that you could share more publicly?

posts.title: Sales ops evolved while nobody was watching
posts.hook:
Sales ops used to mean: clean the CRM, build the reports, fix the broken workflow.

In 2026 it means: architect the entire revenue machine.
posts.body:
Sales ops used to mean: clean the CRM, build the reports, fix the broken workflow.

In 2026 it means: architect the entire revenue machine.

The teams I work with that are growing fastest have a common trait. Their sales ops person isn't buried in Salesforce admin work. They're designing signal-based routing, building enrichment pipelines, and deploying AI agents that handle the research reps used to do manually.

One ops lead I know built a system where every inbound lead gets automatically enriched through 4 data providers, scored against their ICP, matched to the right rep based on territory AND tech stack fit, and delivered with a pre-built research brief. Total time from form fill to rep notification: under 90 seconds.

That used to take a BDR 20 minutes per lead.

The role didn't change titles. It changed altitude. Same person, same Slack channel, wildly different impact.

The ops teams still spending 60% of their time on data hygiene and report building are solving last decade's problem. The ones building autonomous pipeline systems are solving this decade's.

Automation didn't replace sales ops. It promoted it.
posts.cta: Where does your sales ops team spend most of their time right now? Curious if the split has shifted.

inspired_by.author: David Cummings
inspired_by.summary: Explores the evolution of sales ops into strategic partners for GTM, highlighting automation's role in scaling outbound at enterprise levels. Cites examples where sales ops teams drove 2x pipeline growth via ops-led experiments. Stresses hiring generalists who code for modern ops roles.

remixes.label: role-evolution
remixes.hook: The best sales ops person I know hasn't touched a CRM report in 6 months. She builds AI agents.
remixes.body:
The best sales ops person I know hasn't touched a CRM report in 6 months. She builds AI agents.

Her team's pipeline grew 3x while headcount stayed flat. Not because the reps got better at cold calling. Because the ops infrastructure got smarter.

She built an enrichment pipeline that pulls firmographic and technographic data from six sources, scores it against their ICP, and routes the highest-signal accounts directly to reps with pre-written research briefs.

The reps spend zero time on research. Zero time on data entry. Zero time figuring out who to call. The system handles all of it.

This is what sales ops looks like in 2026. Not Salesforce admin work. Not dashboard building. Not cleaning up duplicate records.

Architecting the revenue machine. Designing the signal detection layer. Building the automation that turns raw data into qualified pipeline.

The gap between companies where sales ops is still doing manual CRM work and companies where sales ops is building intelligent systems is getting wider every quarter. And it's showing up directly in pipeline numbers.

The title says "Sales Operations." The actual job description is closer to revenue engineer.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your sales ops team's time goes to building vs. maintaining?

remixes.label: signal-vs-noise
remixes.hook: Your sales team is drowning in data and starving for signals. That's a sales ops problem, not a rep problem.
remixes.body:
Your sales team is drowning in data and starving for signals. That's a sales ops problem, not a rep problem.

Every CRM has thousands of records. Every enrichment tool adds more fields. Every intent data provider adds another signal. Reps have more information available than ever and somehow less clarity on who to call.

The problem isn't data volume. It's signal routing. Someone needs to take all that raw data and turn it into a prioritized, actionable queue. That's the new sales ops job.

The fastest-growing teams I work with have rebuilt their ops layer around this principle. Instead of giving reps access to tools and training them to fish, they built systems that hand reps a fish every morning. Pre-researched accounts. Scored and ranked. With the talking points already written.

The tech stack matters less than the architecture. You can do this with Salesforce or HubSpot or a spreadsheet with Zapier. The point is that someone designed the signal-to-action pipeline.

Most companies have the data. Most companies have the tools. What they're missing is the ops architect who connects the dots and builds the system that turns raw inputs into rep-ready outputs.

That role used to be optional. Now it's the bottleneck.
remixes.cta: How does your team decide which accounts to work each morning? Manual process or automated?

tones.advisor:
The sales ops function has undergone a fundamental shift, and teams that haven't adapted are falling behind in measurable ways.

Traditionally, sales ops meant CRM administration, reporting, and workflow maintenance. Necessary work, but support work. The function existed to keep the existing machine running.

In 2026, the highest-performing teams have repositioned sales ops as the architecture layer of their revenue engine. The people in these roles are designing signal-based routing, building enrichment pipelines that aggregate data from multiple sources, and deploying AI agents that handle the research and preparation reps used to do manually.

My recommendation if you're leading a revenue team: audit how your sales ops team spends their time. If more than 50% goes to maintenance, report building, or manual data work, you have a structural problem.

The reallocation should go toward: building automated enrichment and scoring, designing signal-based account routing, and creating systems that hand reps pre-researched, prioritized accounts each morning.

The teams executing this well are seeing pipeline growth without proportional headcount growth. That's the leverage sales ops should be creating. Not cleaner reports. Better signal routing.
tones.provocateur:
If your sales ops team is still primarily building reports and cleaning CRM data, you're running a 2019 operation in 2026.

Harsh? Maybe. But the numbers don't lie. The teams crushing pipeline growth right now have sales ops people who look more like engineers than analysts. They're building enrichment pipelines, deploying AI agents for research, and designing signal-based routing that tells reps exactly who to call and why.

Meanwhile, most sales ops teams are stuck in Salesforce admin purgatory. Fixing broken workflows. Building dashboards nobody looks at. Deduplicating records.

That's not sales ops. That's maintenance.

The uncomfortable question: if your sales ops team disappeared for a month, would pipeline actually suffer? Or would reps just use slightly messier data?

Now ask the same question about the teams who've rebuilt ops as an intelligence layer. If their enrichment pipeline, their scoring models, their automated research briefs went away, reps would be paralyzed.

That's the difference between ops as overhead and ops as infrastructure. One is a cost center that everyone tolerates. The other is the reason the revenue machine works at all.

posts.title: Browser agents are underrated
posts.hook: Everyone's excited about coding agents. The browser agents are going to be bigger.
posts.body:
Everyone's excited about coding agents. The browser agents are going to be bigger.

I run Playwright-based automations for scraping, testing, and workflow execution. The gap between what a browser agent could do 12 months ago and what it can do now is staggering.

Last week I built a competitive intel pipeline that opens a prospect's website, reads their pricing page, extracts their positioning language, compares it against 5 competitors, and generates a differentiation brief. All browser-based. No API. No scraping service. Just a headless browser with an AI reading the page like a human would.

The infrastructure challenge is real though. Session management is fragile. Login states expire. CAPTCHAs appear randomly. Rate limiting is inconsistent. Every site handles auth differently.

But these are solvable engineering problems, not fundamental limitations.

The teams building browser-native AI workflows right now are going to have a massive advantage in 12 months. Every SaaS tool that doesn't have an API becomes accessible. Every manual browser workflow becomes automatable.

Coding agents get the attention because developers are the audience. Browser agents get the value because business workflows live in the browser.
posts.cta: What browser-based workflow would you automate first if reliability wasn't an issue?

inspired_by.author: Addy Osmani
inspired_by.summary: Covers advancements in browser-based AI agents for web automation and dev tools. Discusses infrastructure challenges like latency in agent orchestration and proposes hybrid edge-cloud solutions. Includes code snippets for prototyping.

remixes.label: real-world-pipeline
remixes.hook: I built a competitive intel pipeline last week that would have taken a junior analyst 40 hours. It runs in 12 minutes.
remixes.body:
I built a competitive intel pipeline last week that would have taken a junior analyst 40 hours. It runs in 12 minutes.

The workflow: Playwright opens a prospect's website, navigates to their pricing page, extracts positioning language and tier structure, screenshots the page, then hits their blog for recent content themes. All automated. All repeatable.

Then it enriches that data with LinkedIn activity from their leadership team, cross-references with job postings to detect hiring signals, and compiles everything into a structured brief that lands in my CRM.

12 minutes from trigger to deliverable. No manual research. No copy-pasting between tabs.

This is what browser agents enable that API-based agents can't: interacting with the web the way a human does. Most business data isn't available through APIs. It's on web pages. Pricing pages, about pages, blog archives, job boards, LinkedIn profiles.

The reliability has improved dramatically in the last year. Playwright with AI-assisted selectors handles layout changes that would have broken old scrapers. Error recovery is built in. The agents retry, adapt, and flag when something looks wrong instead of silently failing.

Coding agents get the hype because developers are the loudest audience. Browser agents will get the adoption because every business function needs web-based research.
remixes.cta: What research workflow eats the most time on your team right now? Odds are a browser agent could handle it.

remixes.label: why-browsers-win
remixes.hook: 90% of useful business data has no API. It lives on web pages. That's why browser agents matter more than coding agents.
remixes.body:
90% of useful business data has no API. It lives on web pages. That's why browser agents matter more than coding agents.

Pricing pages. Job postings. LinkedIn profiles. Blog archives. Competitor positioning. Customer reviews. Conference speaker lists. Press releases.

None of this is in a database you can query. All of it is on a web page you can scrape.

Coding agents are powerful, but they serve developers. Browser agents serve every function in a company. Sales needs competitive research. Marketing needs content analysis. Recruiting needs candidate sourcing. Product needs user feedback aggregation.

I've been running Playwright-based browser automations for months now. The capability jump in the last year is significant. AI-assisted element selection means the agent adapts when page layouts change. Built-in retry logic handles flaky pages. Structured output means the data lands in your systems clean.

Last week I built a pipeline that scrapes a prospect's entire web presence, their site, their team's LinkedIn activity, their job postings, and compiles a competitive brief automatically. Start to finish: 12 minutes.

The equivalent manual process takes a junior analyst half a day per prospect.

Browser agents aren't exciting to demo at conferences. They're not generating viral Twitter threads. But they're quietly becoming the most practical AI automation for actual business operations.
remixes.cta: What manual web research does your team do repeatedly that you'd love to automate?

I'd encourage anyone building AI automation to look seriously at browser agents. They're less discussed than coding agents, but the practical business applications are broader.

Here's why: most valuable business intelligence lives on web pages, not in APIs. Competitor pricing, hiring signals from job postings, leadership team activity on LinkedIn, content themes from blogs. None of this is available through structured endpoints.

Browser agents, built on tools like Playwright, interact with web pages the way a human researcher would. Navigate, read, extract, move to the next source. The difference is speed and consistency.

I run Playwright-based automations for competitive intel, and the reliability improvement over the past 12 months has been substantial. AI-assisted selectors adapt to layout changes. Error recovery is built in. The agents handle edge cases that would have required manual intervention a year ago.

My recommendation: identify the manual web research workflows on your team that happen repeatedly. Prospect research, competitive analysis, market monitoring. These are ideal candidates for browser automation.

Start with one workflow. Build it with Playwright. Measure the time savings against the manual process. Most teams find the ROI is obvious within the first week.

The coding agent hype will settle. The browser agent adoption is just starting.

The AI community is obsessed with coding agents while ignoring the category that will actually transform business operations.

Browser agents. The boring ones. The ones that open web pages and extract data instead of writing code.

Think about it. How many people in your company write code? Maybe 20%. How many do web-based research? Nearly 100%.

Sales reps research prospects on LinkedIn. Marketers analyze competitor content. Recruiters source candidates. Product managers read user reviews. Everyone spends hours clicking through web pages, copying data, and pasting it somewhere useful.

That's the automation opportunity. Not helping developers write code slightly faster. Eliminating the manual web research that every business function drowns in.

I built a competitive intel pipeline last week with Playwright. It scrapes pricing pages, extracts positioning, pulls LinkedIn activity, checks job postings for hiring signals. 12 minutes. The manual version takes a junior analyst 40 hours.

But nobody's tweeting about this because "I automated web scraping" doesn't get engagement like "my AI agent wrote a React app."

The developer audience is loud. The business automation market is large. Browser agents are going to win on market size, not mindshare.

I want to make a case for the most underrated category in AI right now: browser agents.

Coding agents get all the attention because developers are the loudest audience online. But browser agents are going to have broader adoption because they solve a problem every business function has: manual web research.

I built a competitive intel pipeline last week using Playwright. It opens prospect websites, reads pricing pages, extracts positioning, checks LinkedIn activity, and compiles a structured brief. Total runtime: 12 minutes. The manual version used to take 40 hours.

The reliability has improved dramatically in the past year. These aren't the fragile scrapers that break whenever a page layout changes. AI-assisted selectors adapt automatically.

More on what I'm building and how below.
